We find a substantial number of corner choices made. By a corner choice we mean a round of the experiment where the subject allocates 0 tokens to one of the assets. We find that 49 percent of participants make corner choices in all rounds of the experiment and 91 percent make at least one corner choice. These numbers seem consistent with convex time budget experiment of Andreoni and Sprenger (2012b) where 37 percent of subjects make only corner solutions. 16

## 4 Utility Functions and Revealed Preference Tests

The goal of this section is to explain the revealed preference techniques used in our analysis. We shall only be interested in utility functions which are continuous and increasing and so we embed this into the definition of utility.

Definition 1. A utility function is a strictly increasing and continuous function U : R 4 + → R .

## 4.1 Quantifying Imperfect Utility Maximization

An e -utility maximizer is an agent who possesses some utility function U and some number e ∈ [0 , 1] so that when choosing from budget set B ( p, w ) = { c ∈ R 4 + : p · c ≤ w } ,

16 It seems reasonable that simply having more commodities would make corner solutions more likely. The experiment of Andreoni and Sprenger (2012b) has only two goods (there is no risk) and so the presence of fewer all-corner solutions may be due to the fact that they have fewer goods.

selects a bundle ¯ c ∈ B ( p, w ) that satisfy

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Such an e is said to be the agent's efficiency level . Of course, an agent who maximizes U with e = 1 is a utility-maximizing agent in the familiar sense.

Given a data set of demand observations, we could ask if it is consistent with utilitymaximization at a given efficiency level. This is captured by the following definition.

Definition 2. Suppose c 1 , . . . , c N are the bundles chosen by a subject in rounds 1 through N of the experiment and let p 1 , . . . , p N be the corresponding price vectors. We refer to the collection O = { ( c 1 , p 1 ) , . . . , ( c N , p N ) } as a dataset . The dataset O is rationalized by utility function U at efficiency level e if the choices made could have been made by an e -utility maximizer with utility function U . That is, for all n

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

A dataset O that admits such a utility function U is said to be e -rationalizable .

Obviously, if a dataset O is e -rationalizable then it is also e ′ -rationalizable for any e ′ &lt; e . The critical cost efficiency index (see Afriat (1972)) is defined as

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

## 4.2 Utility functions satisfying further properties

Let P denote a property which a utility function may or may not possess 17 and let e ∈ [0 , 1]. Consider the choices of one subject from our experiment. We would like to develop a way to answer the following question: does there exist a utility function U satisfying P that rationalizes the choices of the subject at efficiency level e ? We focus on properties P which can be represented by a preorder. 18

Definition 3. Let /trianglerightequal be a preorder and let U be a utility function. Utility function U agrees with /trianglerightequal if

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

17 Formally, one may take P to be a set of utility functions. Then, when we say U possesses property P we just mean U ∈ P .

18 A preorder is a transitive and reflexive binary relation.

While it can be difficult to determine if a subject's choices can be rationalized by a utility function that agrees with some arbitrary preorder /trianglerightequal , we shall see that when the preorder satisfies the following property the task is greatly simplified.

Definition 4. A preorder /trianglerightequal is a composable preorder if for all c, c ′ ∈ R 4 +

there exists ¯ c so that c /trianglerightequal ¯ c ≥ c ′ ⇐⇒ there exists ˜ c so that c ≥ ˜ c /trianglerightequal c ′

where ≥ is the product order on R 4 + .

Note that, by definition a utility function is an increasing function and thus it must agree with the product order ≥ ; what composability guarantees is that the added property /trianglerightequal interacts nicely with ≥ . We impose two other, relatively innocuous conditions (in addition to the composable condition), on all preorders considered in this paper and refer to a preorder which satisfies all three conditions as well-behaved . A full definition of a well-behaved preorder can be found in the Appendix (Section A.1).

We now present two key preference properties studied in this paper; each property is captured by agreement with a particular well-behaved preorder. It is convenient to denote the consumption bundle of an agent by c = ( c 1 , c 2 ), where c 1 = ( c 11 , c 12 ) denotes state 1 consumption (at dates 1 and 2) and c 2 = ( c 21 , c 22 ) denotes state 2 consumption.

Lottery Equivalence This property says that swapping the consumption streams in states 1 and 2 makes no difference to utility. It makes sense if the realized state has no intrinsic significance for the agent's preference. Thus the bundles ( a, b ) and ( b, a ) would both simply correspond to a lottery that pays out streams a and b with equal probability and thus must give the same utility.

Definition 5. A utility function U : R 4 + → R satisfies lottery equivalence if

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

The lottery equivalence property can be re-stated as an agreement with a preorder /trianglerightequal . The lottery equivalence preorder /trianglerightequal LE is defined as follows:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

for all a, b ∈ R 2 + . It is straightforward to check that /trianglerightequal LE is a preorder and that it is composable (and in fact it is also well-behaved). The asymmetric part of /trianglerightequal LE is empty: there are no elements c and c ′ such that c /triangleright LE c ′ . The lottery equivalence property for a

utility function U can be equivalently stated as follows: U ( c ) ≥ U ( c ′ ) whenever c /trianglerightequal LE c ′ .

Impatience. In models of time preference, it is common to postulate that agents are impatient, which means that an agent has a preference for receiving sooner payments over later payments. When confined to a consumption stream with no risk (in our context, this would be a bundle of the form (( a, b ) , ( a, b ))), the property says that U (( a, b ) , ( a, b )) ≥ U (( b, a ) , ( b, a )) if a ≥ b . A stronger version of this property is to require impatience to hold state-by-state, which is captured by the following definition.

Definition 6. The utility function U : R 4 + → R satisfies impatience if

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

whenever any of the following hold:

- (i) a ≥ b , ˜ a = b , ˜ b = a , and ( a ′ , b ′ ) = (˜ a ′ , ˜ b ′ );
- (ii) a ′ ≥ b ′ , ˜ a ′ = b ′ , ˜ b ′ = a ′ , and ( a, b ) = (˜ a, ˜ b ); and
- (iii) a ≥ b , a ′ ≥ b ′ , ˜ a = b , ˜ b = a , and ˜ a ′ = b ′ , ˜ b ′ = a ′ . 19

We define /trianglerightequal I as follows: (( a, b ) , ( a ′ , b ′ )) /trianglerightequal I ((˜ a, ˜ b ) , (˜ a ′ , ˜ b ′ )) if either (( a, b ) , ( a ′ , b ′ )) = ((˜ a, ˜ b ) , (˜ a ′ , ˜ b ′ )) or (i), (ii) or (iii) in Definition 6 holds. It is straightforward to check that /trianglerightequal I is composable (indeed, well-behaved) preorder with a nonempty asymmetric part, 20 and a utility function U satisfies impatience if and only if U ( c ) ≥ U ( c ′ ) whenever c /trianglerightequal I c ′ .

Lottery Equivalence and Impatience. Both lottery equivalence and impatience are natural properties in our choice environment and it would be useful to check if a dataset can be rationalized by a utility function with both properties. This corresponds to requiring that U agrees with the preorder /trianglerightequal LEI , which is the transitive closure of /trianglerightequal LE and /trianglerightequal I . 21 Our testing procedure is applicable so long as /trianglerightequal LEI is a well-behaved preorder. It turns out that that is indeed the case.

There are other properties studied in this paper which can be defined as agreement with a well-behaved preorder. Furthermore, these preorders can also be combined, via transitive closure, while remaining well-behaved (see Appendix (Sections A.2 and A.3) for the details). This means that we have the ability to test for e -rationalization by a

19 (i) corresponds to the case where utility is increased if the state 1 consumption stream is altered from ( b, a ) to ( a, b ), which brings forward the larger consumption and postpones the smaller consumption, while keeping state 2 consumption fixed. Case (ii) corresponds to the case where the change takes place in state 2, while (iii) corresponds to the case where consumption in both states are altered.

20 For example, ((2 , 1) , c 2 ) /triangleright ((1 , 2) , c 2 ) for any c 2 .

21 We write c /trianglerightequal + c ′ if either c /trianglerightequal LE c ′ or c /trianglerightequal I c ′ . By definition, c /trianglerightequal LEI c ′ if there exists some finite sequence c 1 , . . . , c K so that c /trianglerightequal + c 1 /trianglerightequal + c 2 /trianglerightequal + . . . /trianglerightequal + c K /trianglerightequal + c ′ .

utility function with any combination of such properties.

In our experiment the two states are equally likely. In this case, the utility function U : R 4 + → R has the discounted expected utility (DEU) form if

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

for some strictly increasing function u : R + → R and δ ∈ (0 , 1]. It is clear that with this form, both lottery equivalence and impatience holds. However, this is by no means the only case where these properties are satisfied; we postpone the discussion of other functional forms with these properties to Section 4.4.

## 4.3 Revealed Preference and GARP

We refer to a dataset O = { ( c 1 , p 1 ) , . . . , ( c N , p N ) } as e -rationalizable/trianglerightequal if it can be rationalized by a utility function that agrees with /trianglerightequal at efficiency level e . Clearly, if O is e -rationalizable/trianglerightequal for some e then O is e ′ -rationalizable/trianglerightequal for any e ′ &lt; e . Our objective is to devise a way to calculate the (generalized) critical cost efficiency index

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Note that ¯ e as defined by (4) must satisfy ¯ e ≥ ¯ e ( /trianglerightequal ).

Let I /trianglerightequal ( c ) be the set of bundles ranked weakly better than c under the /trianglerightequal preorder. That is

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Given O , let C = { c 1 , . . . , c N } . We define two binary relations on C .

Definition 7. Let /trianglerightequal be a preorder, e ∈ [0 , 1], and O = { ( c 1 , p 1 , ) , . . . , ( c N , p N ) } be a dataset. For c n , c m ∈ C write c n /trianglerightequal ∗ e c m if there exists ¯ c ∈ I /trianglerightequal ( c m ) so that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

If ≥ can be replaced by its strict version in (9) then we write c n /triangleright ∗ e c m . 22 We refer to /trianglerightequal ∗ e and /triangleright ∗ e as, respectively, the revealed preferred and strictly revealed preferred relations.

If a subject is truly an e -utility maximizer with a utility function that agrees with preorder /trianglerightequal , then c n /trianglerightequal ∗ e c m implies U ( c n ) ≥ U ( c m ) and c n /triangleright ∗ e c m implies U ( c n ) &gt; U ( c m ).

22 Some authors use /triangleright ∗ e to represent the asymmetric part of the preorder /trianglerightequal ∗ e . That is c /triangleright ∗ e c ′ if c /trianglerightequal ∗ e c ′ and not c ′ /trianglerightequal ∗ e c . Note that our notation differs from this usage.

Thus, the following property must hold for such a subject.

Definition 8. A dataset O = { ( c 1 , p 1 , ) , . . . , ( c N , p N ) } satisfies the e -Generalized Axiom of Revealed Preference according to /trianglerightequal ( e -GARP/trianglerightequal ) 23 if the revealed preference relations contain no strict cycles in the following sense: for any sequence n 1 , n 2 , . . . , n K where

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

it is not the case that c n K /triangleright ∗ e c n 1 .

The following proposition is the theoretical result underpinning the testing procedures in this paper. It relates the notion of e -GARP/trianglerightequal to that of rationalizing O with a utility function that agrees with /trianglerightequal .

Proposition 1. Let /trianglerightequal be a well-behaved preorder and let O = { ( c 1 , p 1 ) , . . . , ( c N , p N ) } be a dataset. Then ¯ e ( /trianglerightequal ) (as defined by (7)) satisfies

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

This proposition states that the critical cost efficiency index ¯ e ( /trianglerightequal ) equals the supremum of those efficiency levels e at which e -GARP/trianglerightequal holds. Determining the latter is straightforward once the revealed preference relations have been computed, since there are well known algorithms for checking the absence of strict cycles. Working out the revealed preference relations in turn require determining the set I /trianglerightequal ( c ) (defined in (8)). Crucially, for the preorders considered in this paper, this set is finite (see Appendix, Sections A.2 and A.3). For example, it is clear that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Writing out I /trianglerightequal I ( a, b ) or I /trianglerightequal LEI ( a, b ) is a bit more longwinded but it is not difficult and, plainly, the set is finite. In the Appendix (Section A.4) we provide a systematic way of working out I /trianglerightequal ( c ) for a general class of preorders /trianglerightequal that contain all those considered in this paper. The bottomline is that, with Proposition 1, we now have a workable way of computing the critical cost efficiency index.

23 The term 'generalized axiom of revealed preference' as well as its acronym GARP is from the treatment of Afriat's Theorem in Varian (1982). Our concept generalizes the original definition; this is discussed in greater detail in Section 4.3.2.

## 4.3.1 Proof of Proposition 1

The proof of Proposition 1 is found in full in the Appendix (Section A.1). It uses Theorem 2 in NOQ and here we outline the connection between the two results.

Given a preorder /trianglerightequal , let /trianglerightequal ·≥ be the transitive closure of /trianglerightequal and ≥ . 24 NOQ's result uses the binary relation /trianglerightequal /star e , where c n /trianglerightequal /star e c m if there exists ¯ c ∈ I /trianglerightequal ·≥ ( c m ) so that (9) holds. One may state NOQ's Theorem 2 in our context as saying the following: the choices of a subject can be rationalized (at efficiency level e ) by a utility function U that agrees with /trianglerightequal if and only if /trianglerightequal /star e is strict cycle free in the sense of Definition 8. 25

Notice that straightforwardly applying NOQ's result would require checking if c n /trianglerightequal /star e c m , which may be problematic because the set I /trianglerightequal ·≥ ( c ) has infinitely many members. The contribution of Proposition 1 is to establish that /trianglerightequal /star e and /trianglerightequal ∗ e are identical when /trianglerightequal is composable , and /trianglerightequal ∗ e is easier to check because I /trianglerightequal ( c ) is a finite set in many applications. In other words, when the preorder is composable, NOQ's Theorem 2 becomes fully operational as a revealed preference test. 26

## 4.3.2 Afriat's Theorem

Proposition 1 delivers a version of Afriat's Theorem as a special case. If one chooses /trianglerightequal as the trivial equality preorder (by definition, c /trianglerightequal ¯ c if c = ¯ c ) then the revealed preference relation in Definition 7 reduces to c n /trianglerightequal ∗ e c m if either ep n · c n ≥ p n · c m or c n ≥ c m , and the acyclicality condition in Definition 8 reduces to GARP (at efficiency level e ) as it is usually defined. Thus Proposition 1 with the equality preorder recovers the known result that the critical cost efficiency index ¯ e (as defined by (4)) is equal to the supremum of those efficiency levels at which GARP holds. 27

## 4.4 Functional Form Restrictions on Utility

We have already observed that when U : R 4 + → R has the DEU form, both the lottery equivalence and impatience properties are satisfied. More generally, lottery equivalence holds if U is weakly separable across states, i.e., there is a strictly increasing function f : R 2 + → R and a strictly increasing and symmetric function G : R ( f ) → R (where

24 Let a ¯ /trianglerightequal b if either a /trianglerightequal b or a ≥ b . The transitive closure of /trianglerightequal and ≥ is the binary relation /trianglerightequal + , where c /trianglerightequal + c ′ if there is a sequence c 1 , . . . , c K so that c ¯ /trianglerightequal c 1 ¯ /trianglerightequal c 2 ¯ /trianglerightequal . . . ¯ /trianglerightequal c K ¯ /trianglerightequal c ′ .

25 A nuance is that the strict relations /triangleright ∗ e and /triangleright /star e are defined slightly differently. This is explained in the proof of Lemma 2 in the Appendix.

26 Polisson, Quah, and Renou (2017) use Theorem 2 in NOQ to test for symmetry and other properties of the utility function. The algorithm they employ can be thought of as an application of Proposition 1, in the sense that the preorders they impose are all composable.

27 For a direct proof of this claim see Halevy et al. (2018).

R ( f ) denotes the range of f ) such that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

This is known as a weakly separable form because for an agent with such a utility, the agent's preference between (( a, b ) , c 2 ) and ((¯ a, ¯ b ) , c 2 )) hinges only on whether f ( a, b ) is smaller or greater than f (¯ a, ¯ b ) and is independent of the consumption stream ( c 2 ) in state 2. Hence, for an agent with such a preference, it makes sense to speak of the agent's preference over the consumption stream realized in state s , without reference to consumption in other states. We shall refer to a utility function taking the form in (10) as state-separable .

Note that state-separable is not implied by lottery equivalence. For example,

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

obeys lottery equivalence but cannot be expressible in the form (10). 28 Lottery equivalence also holds if U is separable across time, i.e., there are strictly increasing functions H and g , with g symmetric, such that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

For the functional form (10), impatience holds if f has the property that f ( a, b ) ≥ f ( b, a ) whenever a ≥ b . For example, impatience holds if f ( a, b ) = u ( a ) + δu ( b ) where u is strictly increasing and continuous, and δ ∈ (0 , 1]. In the case of (11), impatience is satisfied if h (and f ) has the property that h ( a, b ) ≥ h ( b, a ) whenever a ≥ b .

An important special case of (10) is the Kihlstrom-Mirman form (after the representation in Kihlstrom and Mirman (1974)), where there are strictly increasing functions φ and u and a number δ &gt; 0 such that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

This form obeys lottery equivalence and, if δ ∈ (0 , 1], impatience as well.

28 Notice that the ranking between two state 1 consumption streams, say ( a, b ) and (¯ a, ¯ b ), clearly depends on the state 2 consumption stream ( a ′ , b ′ ).

## 5 Experimental Results

In this section, we use the NOQ method (as captured by Proposition 1) to test different restrictions on the utility function against the experimental data. We consider several different models, but all of them are weaker than discounted expected utility. By looking at the variation in their performances, we aim to establish precisely which features of the DEU model are less supported by observed choices.

We begin by addressing the first and most elementary issue: are the subjects maximizing any utility function?

## 5.1 Testing For Utility Maximization

Table 2: Pass Rates for Utility Maximization

|                         |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 99 |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 95 |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 90 |
|-------------------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| Experimental subjects   |           71.8 |           90.3 |           97.1 |
| Uniform random datasets |            0   |            0   |            0   |
| Resampled datasets      |           24.3 |           38.6 |           65.7 |

We first test how the participants in the experiments are behaving as imperfect utility-maximizers by calculating their efficiency indices. The basic utility-maximization model appears to perform very well, with virtually everyone having critical cost efficiency indices that exceed 0.9, and more than 70 percent behaving in a way almost indistinguishable from exact utility-maximizers. (See the first row of Table 2.)

To remove the possibility that this is due to an overly lax experimental environment, we use two methods to check the power of our experiment. In both methods, we created 10,000 simulated datasets (each of which has 41 observations) and calculated their efficiency indices. In the first method we generate observations using uniformly random allocations in which the expended tokens sum up to 100. In the second method, we first pool the decisions made by all subjects in the experiment and then generate datasets (with 41 observations each) by sampling from this set. The results for the first and second method are reported in the second and third rows of Table 2. A vanishingly small number of datasets generated by the first method are consistent with utility-maximization at any reasonable efficiency threshold; for random datasets generated via the second method, the efficiency indices are higher but still not at all close to the results from the (actual) experimental datasets.

Together these results give strong support to the hypothesis that subjects are maximizing some utility function. Let us now move on to test what properties these utility

functions are likely to satisfy.

## 5.2 Three properties of the DEU Model

In this subsection, we focus on three properties of the discounted expected utility model. It is clear that a discounted expected utility function satisfies impatience and lottery equivalence. But this utility form has a property stronger than lottery equivalence: it also satisfies correlation neutrality.

Definition 9. A utility function U : R 4 + → R satisfies correlation neutrality if

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

for all ( a, b ) and ( a ′ , b ′ ) in R 2 + .

Notice that equations (14) and (15) together imply lottery equivalence so correlation neutrality is a stronger property than lottery equivalence. Correlation neutrality requires a subject to be indifferent between a lottery that (say) pays out consumption streams of (0 , 10) and (5 , 0) with equal probability and another that pays out (5 , 10) and (0 , 0) with equal probability: the fact that the second lottery involves correlation, in the sense that both the favorable payouts occur in the same state, does not bother the subject. Plainly, correlation neutrality holds in the DEU model and, more generally, it holds when the utility function is separable across time (see (12)).

Correlation neutrality can also be tested using Proposition 1 via an appropriately chosen preorder (see Appendix). Table 3 reports the performance of four properties (lottery equivalence, impatience, their combination, and lastly correlation neutrality) as measured by their respective efficiency indices. Notice that, for all of these models, the pass rates at a given efficiency threshold is lower than that for (basic) utilitymaximization, which is as it should be, since the models tested in Table 2 are all more stringent.

Table 3: Pass Rates for DEU properties

|                          |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 99 |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 95 |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 90 |
|--------------------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| Lottery Equivalence (LE) |           62.1 |           84.5 |           93.2 |
| Impatience               |           65.1 |           84.5 |           92.2 |
| LE and Impatience        |           55.3 |           79.6 |           91.3 |
| Correlation Neutrality   |           14.6 |           22.3 |           56.3 |

## 5.2.1 Lottery Equivalence

We see from Table 3 that a large proportion of subjects display behavior roughly consistent with the maximization of a lottery equivalent utility function. Imposing lottery equivalence must mean lower pass rates compared to the basic utility-maximization model, but the drop in pass rates are quite modest. For example, while 84 . 5 percent of our experiment participants have an efficiency index above 0 . 95 for lottery equivalent utility maximization, the corresponding percentage for utility-maximization (see Table 2) is 90.3 percent. (The efficiency index of an additional six participants fall below 0.95 when lottery equivalence is imposed.)

Lottery equivalence may seem like a very reasonable property, but the imposition of this condition has strong implications over and above that from utility-maximization. In particular, a violation of this property can occur with just a single observation, something that is not possible with utility-maximization (where violations can only be observed with multiple observations). As an example we consider subject 10. Subject 10 has a basic efficiency index of 0 . 976 but it drops to 0 . 917 when lottery equivalence is imposed. Why has this occurred? Examining even the subject's very first decision is instructive. In observation 1 the subject faces p 1 = ((1 . 25 , 2) , (1 , 0 . 8)) and purchases c 1 = ((48 , 0) , (40 , 0)). This violates lottery equivalence since the subject could instead have purchased the bundle ((40 , 0) , (50 , 0)), which any lottery equivalent utility-maximizer would strictly prefer to c 1 .

The behavior exhibited by participant 10 is not rare: we find that almost 40 percent of subjects would (at some observation) purchase a bundle which would have been cheaper to buy by merely swapping the states in which consumption was taking place (thus freeing up tokens for more consumption). Of course, these violations, while fairly numerous, usually involve only small cost inefficiencies, which is why the efficiency indices across the experiment population remain high.

We view the fact that 84 . 5 percent of participants have an efficiency index in excess of 0 . 95 for lottery equivalence as convincing evidence in favor of the notion that most participants have preferences satisfying this property. A potential objection to this claim would be to assert that our test lacks power to properly reject the notion of lottery equivalent utility maximization. To provide evidence against this claim we introduce a new restriction on utility functions which is equally restrictive as lottery equivalence and compare their performance. A utility function U is said to satisfy time neutrality if

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

for any ( a, b ) and ( a ′ , b ′ ). This property is not an implication of DEU model, unless δ = 1.

Since the prices on all four goods are chosen with the same distribution, the correlation neutrality and time neutraility properties must, in formal terms, be equally restrictive. But their performances differ sharply (see Table 4). For example, only 68 . 9 percent of participants have efficiency indices in excess of 0 . 95 for the time neutral property, considerably below the 84 . 5 percent reported for lottery equivalence. To check this rigorously, we apply the one-sided version of the McNemar test (see Fagerland, Lydersen, and Laake (2014)), under the null hypothesis that the probability of a participant having an efficiency index above a certain level for lottery equivalence is the same as that for time neutrality. We report p-values for efficiency thresholds of 0.99, 0.95, and 0.9. We see strong evidence that the pass rates at a given efficiency threshold is different for the two tests.

Table 4: Pass Rates: Lottery Equivalence vs Time Neutrality

|                       |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 99 |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 95 |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 90 |
|-----------------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| Lottery Equivalence   |         62.1   |           84.5 |           93.2 |
| Time Neutrality       |         56.3   |           68.9 |           79.6 |
| McNemar Test p-values |          0.066 |            0   |            0   |

## 5.2.2 Correlation Neutrality

It is not too much to say that correlation neutrality performs abysmally, with only 22 . 3 percent of participants having a efficiency index score in excess of 0 . 95 (see Table 3). Many previous papers have commented on theoretical grounds on the undesirability of this implication of the DEU model. Fishburn (1970), Richard (1975), and Duffie and Epstein (1992) are examples. Our results give a sense of the degree to which this matters: if we wanted to make the modest claim that half of our participants satisfy correlation neutrality then we would have to allow for them to waste as much as 10 percent of their budget in their purchasing decisions; in contrast, the choice behavior of half of the subject population can be exactly rationalized by lottery equivalent utility functions.

We explore properties that relax correlation neutrality in Section 5.3.

## 5.2.3 Impatience

We find that 84 . 5 percent of subjects have an efficiency index in excess of 0 . 95 (see Table 3). Notice that this the same proportion that satisfy lottery equivalence at the 0 . 95 efficiency threshold (although the subjects in the two groups do not coincide exactly).

As in the case of lottery equivalence, there are violations of impatience that could be established with just one observation. For example, subject 36 has a perfect efficiency level for basic utility maximization but the efficiency level drops to 0 . 9 for impatient utility maximization. As an example of a violation, consider observation 15, where p 15 = ((0 . 5 , 1 . 25) , (0 . 8 , 1)) and the subject purchased c 15 = ((100 , 0) , (0 , 50)). Obviously, the subject could have purchased ((100 , 0) , (50 , 0)) for only 90 tokens which is a bundle that any impatient utility maximizer must consider at least as good as c 15 . We find violations of this type to be infrequent; indeed, 81 . 6 percent of subjects do not have any single-observation violations (such as the one exhibited by subject 36). Overall, the introduction of impatience causes the efficiency index to fall below 0.95 for an additional six subjects.

To provide evidence that our result is not driven by the laxity of the impatience property, we introduce the complementary property of patience. A utility function U satisfies patience if U ( c 1 , c 2 ) ≥ U ( c ′ 1 , c ′ 2 ) whenever impatience requires U ( c 1 , c 2 ) ≤ U ( c ′ 1 , c ′ 2 ) (according to the criteria set out in Definition 6). It is clear that in the context of this experiment, patience and impatience are equally restrictive properties. We find that only 66 . 0 percent of participants have an efficiency index in excess of 0 . 95 for the patience property, which is considerably below the 84 . 4 percent found for the impatience property. The pass rates are reported in Table 5, with the McNemar Test confirming our informal observation on the significance of the difference in pass rates.

Table 5: Pass Rates: Impatience vs Patience

|              |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 99 |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 95 |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 90 |
|--------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| Impatience   |         65.1   |           84.5 |           92.2 |
| Patience     |         50.5   |           66   |           73.8 |
| McNemar Test |          0.001 |            0   |            0   |

## 5.2.4 Joint Test of Lottery Equivalence and Impatience

We now turn to the hypothesis that each agent has a utility function that satisfies both lottery equivalence and impatience. 29 Note that it is quite possible for a subject's behavior (say) to be exactly rationalized by a utility function that obeys impatience and also exactly rationalized by a utility function that obeys lottery equivalence, but for it not to be exactly rationalized by a utility function with both properties. More generally,

29 If two properties can be defined as agreement with two distinct preorders, then a utility function will satisfy both properties if and only if it agrees with the transitive closure of the two preorders. So long as this transitive closure is also a well-behaved preorder, testing can be carried out using Proposition 1. See Section 4.2 and the Appendix (Sections A.2 and A.3) for further explanation.

for a given subject, the efficiency index for the combined property can be strictly lower than the subject's efficiency index for lottery equivalence and for impatience separately.

It follows from this observation that, while the pass rates for lottery equivalence and the pass rates for impatience impose upper bounds on the pass rate for the combined property, it is theoretically possible for the performance of the combined property to be much worse than that for the two properties separately. It turns out that that is not the case. The pass rate for the combined property remains high; in particular, almost 80 percent of subjects have efficiency indices exceeding 0.95 for the combined property (see Table 3).

For the remaining tests in this section, we shall maintain lottery equivalence and impatience as background assumptions. Not only are these properties ubiquitously assumed in the theory and also intuitive, our experimental evidence has confirmed their viability as maintained assumptions.

## 5.2.5 Testing DEU

So far we have discussed our findings on lottery equivalence, correlation neutrality, and impatience. While these properties are implied by the DEU model, they are not sufficient to guarantee the DEU form. For the DEU model to hold, the independence axiom would have to be added to these properties. 30 However, the independence axiom cannot be tested in isolation using Proposition 1, because there is no preorder /trianglerightequal that represents the independence axiom. Nonetheless, it is possible to test the independence axiom jointly with the other properties, since there is a nonparametric test for the DEU model (specifically, to test if U has the form given by (6)) using the method in Polisson et al. (2017). We did not perform that test because it is clear that the model would perform poorly: its pass rates at any efficiency threshold would have to be even lower than that for correlation neutrality.

30 To be specific, we would need the following version (due to Debreu (1959)) of the independence axiom which is appropriate in our context:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

If /followsequal satisfies this property and correlation neutrality, then Theorem 11.1 in Fishburn (1970) shows that /followsequal can be represented by U ( c 1 , 1 , c 1 , 2 , c 2 , 1 , c 2 , 2 ) = u 1 ( c 1 , 1 ) + u 2 ( c 1 , 2 ) + u 1 ( c 2 , 1 ) + u 2 ( c 2 , 2 ) for increasing and continuous functions u 1 and u 2 . From this, it is not difficult to show that u 2 = δu 1 for δ ∈ (0 , 1] if impatience and the following property holds:

```
(( a, d ) , ( a ′ , d )) /followsequal (( b, d ) , ( b ′ , d )) ⇐⇒ (( d, a ) , ( d, a ′ )) /followsequal (( d, b ) , ( d, b ′ )) ∀ a, a ′ , b, b ′ , d ∈ R + .
```

## 5.3 Correlation Attitudes

We know that correlation neutrality is broadly rejected by the data. We now investigate whether there is anything more that we can say about correlation attitudes and, in particular, whether there is support for the correlation aversion property.

Definition 10. Let U : R 4 + → R be a utility function satisfying lottery equivalence. U satisfies correlation aversion if for all payouts a ≥ a ′ and b ≥ b ′

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

If the above inequality is reversed then U is correlation seeking .

Since we assume that U satisfies lottery equivalence, if (16) holds then so does U (( a, b ′ ) , ( a ′ , b )) ≥ U (( a, b ) , ( a ′ , b ′ )). Whether it is this inequality or (16), a subject who satisfies correlation aversion prefers a lottery where high and low payouts occur at each state, over a lottery where all the high payouts are concentrated in one state.

Obviously the DEU model is consistent with correlation aversion since it implies the even stronger property of correlation neutrality. In the case of the Kihlstrom-Mirman model (see (13)), we know from the analysis of Richard (1975) that if φ is concave then U displays correlation aversion.

Table 6 reports the results of our tests of correlation aversion and correlation seeking. Bear in mind that in both cases, these properties are combined with lottery equivalence and impatience in our tests, so the pass rates obtained must be lower than those reported in the third row of Table 3.

Notice that 75.7 percent of subjects have efficiency indices of 0 . 95 or higher; in other words, 75.7 percent of subjects are e -rationalized (for any e ∈ [0 , 0 . 95)) by utility functions that obey (jointly) lottery equivalence, impatience and correlation aversion. This is only marginally lower than the proportion (79 . 6 percent) of those who, at the same efficiency threshold, satisfy lottery equivalence and impatience jointly (see Table 3). Furthermore, in our experimental environment, the correlation seeking hypothesis is formally no more or less restrictive than correlation aversion, and yet it is clear from Table 6 that the pass rate for this feature is significantly lower. Together, these results give strong support to the correlation aversion property.

Another way of comparing the performance of correlation aversion and correlation seeking is to consider the proportion of subjects who have a correlation aversion efficiency level which is higher than their correlation seeking efficiency level. Table 7 shows that 67.96 percent of participants have a higher efficiency level for correlation aversion

Table 6: Pass Rates: Correlation Aversion vs Correlation Seeking

|                      |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 99 |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 95 |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 90 |
|----------------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| Correlation Aversion |           51.5 |           75.7 |           89.3 |
| Coreelation Seeking  |           16.5 |           26.2 |           52.4 |
| McNemar Test         |            0   |            0   |            0   |

compared to correlation seeking while only 5.83 percent of participants have a higher efficiency level for correlation seeking compared to correlation aversion.

Table 7: Efficiency Levels: Correlation Aversion vs Correlation Seeking

| aversion > seeking   | seeking > aversion   | tie     |
|----------------------|----------------------|---------|
| 67.96 %              | 5.83 %               | 26.21 % |

The support we find in our data for correlation aversion as opposed to correlation neutrality suggests that the behavior of our experimental subjects is not well represented by the DEU model, and more generally, by any model that is separable across time (see (12)). On the other hand, there is experimental evidence indicating that a subject's preference for lotteries that pay out at time t are unaffected by the presence of lotteries, with independent realizations, in a future period t ′ , which supports a time-separable utility formulation (see Andreoni et al. (2017)). In fact, it is possible to reconcile these results because there are utility functions that satisfy correlation aversion and are also time-separable when lotteries are independent. We discuss this in greater detail in the Appendix (Section A.5).

## 5.4 Stochastic Impatience

The concept of stochastic impatience has been introduced recently in DeJarnette et al. (2019). Consider the following two lotteries. Lottery C pays out, with equal probability, either 100 dollars in a day's time or 20 dollars in a month. Lottery D pays out, again with equal probability, 20 dollars in a day's time or 100 dollars in one month. It seems reasonable that an 'impatient' person might prefer lottery C to lottery D: in both lotteries, the realized payouts are the same, but lottery C delivers the higher payout sooner. More generally, this preference is captured by the following definition.

Definition 11. A utility function U : R 4 + → R satisfies stochastic impatience 31 if it satisfies lottery equivalence and for all d ≤ b ≤ a ,

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

31 This definition, applicable to consumption streams, is in the Appendix of DeJarnette et al. (2019).

If the above inequality is reversed we say that U satisfies stochastic patience.

Note that stochastic impatience is not implied by impatience as we have defined it, even though it is implied by correlation neutrality and impatience jointly and thus, importantly, it is a feature of the DEU model. Indeed,

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where the first and last equalities follow from the lottery equivalence and the inequality from impatience. In the case of the Kihlstrom-Mirman model, we show that stochastic impatience holds if (and essentially only if) u ( r ) ≥ 0 for all r ≥ 0 and φ has the property that the function Φ given by

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

is a convex function (see Proposition 3 in the Appendix). 32 , 33

Table 8 presents the results of our test of stochastic impatience. We see that 79 . 6 percent of participants possess efficiency indices in excess of 0 . 95 for this property. Given that we are in fact testing for the combined properties of lottery equivalence, impatience and stochastic impatience, this result is as strong as it could be since (see Table 3) the pass rate of lottery equivalence and impatience is also 79 . 6 percent. In other words, every subject in our experiment that can be 0.95-rationalized by a utility function that obeys lottery equivalence and impatience can also be 0.95-rationalized by a utility function that obeys lottery equivalence, impatience and stochastic impatience.

We can also compare the performance of stochastic impatience with the opposite property of stochastic patience. The pass rates for this property are also reported in Table 8 along with the McNemar Test's p-values. If the stochastic impatience test was too lax or underpowered then we should expect the stochastic patience test to yield similar results. This is clearly not the case, even though the results are less stark than those concerning correlation aversion versus correlation seeking.

Table 8: Pass Rates: Stochastic Impatience vs Stochastic Patience

|                       |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 99 |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 95 |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 90 |
|-----------------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| Stochastic Impatience |         53.4   |           79.6 |         91.3   |
| Stochastic Patience   |         42.7   |           68   |         82.5   |
| McNemar Test          |          0.001 |            0   |          0.001 |

We next consider how many subjects have higher efficiency levels for stochastic impatience than stochastic patience. We find that 22.33 percent of subjects have an efficiency level for stochastic impatience than stochastic patience whereas only 2.91 percent of subjects have a higher efficiency level for stochastic patience. These figures are given in Table 9. Clearly, there is a large block of participants for whom our revealed preference test is unable to distinguish as more in line with stochastic impatience or stochastic patience. However, when the test can distinguish we see that the vast majority of subjects appear to be behaving more in line with stochastic impatience.

Table 9: Efficiency Levels: Stochastic Impatience vs Stochastic Patience

| impatience > patience   | patience > impatience   | tie     |
|-------------------------|-------------------------|---------|
| 22.33 %                 | 2.91 %                  | 74.76 % |

Theorem 1 in DeJarnette et al. (2019) shows that if preferences satisfy the independence axiom and several other (more innocuous) properties then stochastic impatience implies RSTL (see Section 2 for a discussion of the Risk Seeking over Time Lotteries (RSTL) concept). Given the evidence they find against RSTL they suggest either relaxing independence or relaxing stochastic impatience. 34 As we have seen, a significant number our subjects behave in accordance with stochastic impatience, which suggests that relaxing the independence axiom may be the preferred option for these participants.

## 5.5 Takeaways and Challenges

A sharp picture emerges from the tests in this section. Subjects broadly behave as though they are maximizing a lottery equivalent and impatient utility function. There is very little evidence for correlation neutrality, which is a key implication of discounted expected utility. On the other hand, correlation aversion appears to be a widely-held property. We also find that a sizable proportion of the subjects behave in accordance with stochastic impatience.

The evidence put forth provides useful guidance to researchers working on models of choice over time and risk. The ideal model should allow for (not necessarily require ) correlation aversion and stochastic impatience; it should also be consistent with violations of RSTL (see DeJarnette et al. (2019)) and perhaps permit some limited degree of time separability (see Andreoni et al. (2017)).

34 Our experiment does not allow us to test for RSTL as the property has no testable implications when there are only two time periods.

## 6 Ordinal Dominance and a Parametric Model

In this section we estimate a parameterization of the Kihlstrom-Mirman model of equation (13). 35 Recall that the Kihlstrom-Mirman model implies that preferences are state separable and so we first endeavor to test for this property.

## 6.1 Ordinal Dominance

Suppose we learn that a person prefers to receive 2 dollars today and 2 dollars tomorrow to receiving 3 dollars today. Can we say anything about this person's preference over consumption streams when risk is involved? Perhaps it is reasonable to expect that the person's preference will be unchanged if these two consumption streams are mixed with a third stream; in our notation ((2 , 2) , ( a ′′ , b ′′ )) should be preferred to ((3 , 0) , ( a ′′ , b ′′ )) for any a ′′ , b ′′ ≥ 0. The notion that a person's preference over lotteries of consumption streams ought to respect the same person's preferences over non-stochastic consumption streams is the basic idea behind the concept of ordinal dominance as defined in Chew and Epstein (1990).

Definition 12. A utility function U : R 4 + → R satisfies ordinal dominance if U satisfies lottery equivalence and

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

for all a, a ′ , a ′′ , b, b ′ , b ′′ ∈ R + .

Notice that any utility function U also induces a preference over non-stochastic consumption streams. This preference is represented by the increasing and continuous function f : R 2 + → R , given by

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Ordinal dominance is equivalent to U being state-separable, i.e., has the form given by (10), with the sub-utility over consumption streams given by f (as defined by (19)). (See Proposition 4 in the Appendix.)

35 Note that the Kihlstrom-Mirman model, while useful for estimating separate coefficients for risk and time preferences, has the drawback that when it satisfies stochastic impatience it also satisfies the property of risk seeking over time lotteries, a property challenged by experimental findings in DeJarnette et al. (2019). Notwithstanding this evidence, we still believe the estimation of the parameters of the Kihlstrom-Mirman model a useful exercise as it allows for comparison to previous studies in which measures of risk aversion and intertemporal substitution have been estimated.

The NOQ method does not allow us to test weak separability as such, essentially because one could not define an underlying preorder on R 4 + for which consistency with that order is equivalent to weak separability. However, we can check for a simple but striking implication of weak separability.

Suppose an agent's utility is weakly separable across states, with the form (10), and that ˆ c = (ˆ c 1 , ˆ c 2 ) maximizes utility at the price vector ˆ p = (ˆ p 1 , ˆ p 2 ). Then

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

In other words, if (ˆ c 1 , ˆ c 2 ) maximizes overall utility, then ˆ c 1 must maximize the sub-utility in state 1, among consumption streams that cost no more than ˆ c 1 : if this were false then an alternative consumption stream ˜ c 1 is available that raises state 1 sub-utility while not affecting state 2 sub-utility (since ˜ c 1 costs no more than ˆ c 1 ) and hence (ˆ c 1 , ˆ c 2 ) is not overall optimal. By the same argument, ˆ c 2 maximizes state 2 sub-utility, among all consumption streams in state 2 that cost no more than ˆ c 2 .

This observation guarantees that a necessary (but not sufficient) condition for O to be rationalized by a utility function that satisfies impatience and is weakly separable across states is that the dataset O split , given by

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

can be rationalized by some strictly increasing continuous function f : R 2 + → R such that f ( a, b ) ≥ f ( b, a ) if a ≥ b . ( O split is the two-commodity dataset formed by splitting each observation in O into two observations and then combining them into a new dataset.) The efficiency index for this property can be straightforwardly calculated using the NOQ method.

We find that 78 . 6 percent of participants have a efficiency index of at least 0 . 95. (See the first row of Table 10.) Since this is based on a test on the reconstructed dataset O split , it does not nest the tests of lottery equivalence and impatience, which are tested on O . To make the numbers comparable we report on the percent of subjects who have an efficiency index for ordinal dominance above 0 . 95 and have an efficiency index for lottery equivalence with impatience also above 0 . 95. So, for example, 72 . 8 percent of the subjects pass both necessary tests of ordinal dominance at the 0.95 efficiency threshold. Overall, these numbers are at least suggestive that a model with ordinal dominance can describe well the behavior we observe.

Table 10: Ordinal Dominance Test

|                                      |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 99 |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 95 |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 90 |
|--------------------------------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| Impatient subutility                 |           65   |           78.6 |           84.5 |
| Impatient subutility & LE+Impatience |           46.6 |           72.8 |           83.5 |

## 6.2 A Parametric and a Semi-parametric model

In this section we estimate two models. First, we estimate a semi-parametric model which uses discounted utility with a power felicity function to evaluate timed payouts within states and a general symmetric aggregator to aggregate utility across states. That is the bundle c = ( c 1 , 1 , c 1 , 2 , c 2 , 1 , c 2 , 2 ) is evaluated by some utility function U with form

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where G is some increasing, continuous, and symmetric aggregator function and γ ∈ [0 , 1). 36 While this model places a lot of structure on how the subject trades off timed payouts within a state it does not place parametric assumptions on how a subject evaluates risky payouts and does not even require that risky payouts be evaluated using expected utility.

Second, we estimate a parametric version of the Kihlstrom-Mirman model (see equation (13)) where we assume that u and φ are power functions or φ is the log function. That is, we assume that U can be represented either by

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where γ ∈ [0 , 1), η ∈ [0 , ∞ ) \{ 1 } , and δ ∈ [0 , 1] or that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Since the function φ in (13) is concave in (22) and (23), correlation aversion is embedded in our specification (see Richard (1975)). Note that our semi-parametric model nests the parametric model and that the two models coincide for non-risky temporal streams of consumption.

Recall that stochastic impatience holds if (and, in a sense, only if) the function Φ (given by (18)) is a convex function. In this case, if U has the form (23), then Φ( z ) = z ,

36 Our restriction of γ to be less than 1 does not appear to restrict any of our estimates. We make this restriction to accommodate the many corner solutions observed. Note that when γ &gt; 1 then 0 1 -γ is either undefined or -∞ .

and thus stochastic impatience holds; if U has the form (22), then

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

in which case stochastic impatience is satisfied if η ∈ (0 , 1).

The parameters in the model (22) and (23) have natural interpretations. Note that when risk is absent (¯ c 1 = c 1 , 1 = c 2 , 1 and ¯ c 1 = c 1 , 2 = c 2 , 2 ) then preferences over ¯ c 1 and ¯ c 2 have constant elasticity of intertemporal substitution with parameter 1 /γ . Thus, γ can be thought of as representing the curvature in the time preference utility function. For the parametric model, when c 1 , 2 = c 2 , 2 = 0 and so we are only considering earlier period consumption then the preferences take the constant relative risk aversion form with relative risk aversion coefficient η (or 1 in the case of (23)). Thus, η represents the curvature of the risk preference utility function.

Table 11: Semi-parametric and Parametric Models

|                       |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 99 |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 95 |   ¯ e ≥ 0 . 90 |
|-----------------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| LE and Impatience     |           55.3 |           79.6 |           91.3 |
| Semi-parametric model |           24.3 |           47.6 |           73.8 |
| Parametric model      |           12.6 |           35.9 |           59.2 |

We estimate the models by selecting parameters that maximize the efficiency index. A closely related approach to parametric estimation was first used in Halevy et al. (2018). 37 The distribution of efficiency indices is reported in Table 11. The proportion exceeding a given threshold are considerably lower than the corresponding numbers for the nonparametric test, but this is to be expected given that we now estimating parametric and semi-parametric models. However, note that both parametric and semiparametric models generally outperform the non-parametric correlation neutral model.

The median annualized discount rate estimated was 0 . 67 for the parametric model and 0 . 85 for the semi-parametric model. These estimates are higher than that in Andreoni and Sprenger (2012b) which was 0 . 41. However, the upper quartile for the estimated discount rate for both models is the complete patience level of 1 . 0. This may reflect the fact that the difference between early and late payment (8 weeks) was not sufficiently long to pick up meaningful discount rates in some subjects. On the other hand our lower quartile for this parameter is 0 . 42 for the parametric and 0 . 35 for the

37 The index used by Halevy et al. (2018) is not exactly Afriat's efficiency index but a related measure due to Varian (1990). Halevy et al. (2018) compare the results from their estimation approach with the one obtained by minimizing the sum of square differences and found that their method performs better. The data in that case consists of portfolio decisions involving risk, with no time dimension.

semi-parametric which are close to the aforementioned rate found by Andreoni and Sprenger (2012b).

Our median estimated time curvature parameter γ is 0 . 1 for the parametric model and 0 . 75 for the semi-parametric model. The lower quartile of our participants have an estimated γ of 0 indicating perfectly linear utility for both models. The top quartile held a moderate value of 0 . 25 for the parametric model and 0 . 23 for the semi-parametric model.

We now turn the estimates of the risk parameter η for the parametric model. The median estimate was 0 . 55. This can be compared to a finding of 0 . 438 found by Choi et al. (2007) in their experiment run with equiprobable states (but where all payments were made on the same date). Note that Choi et al. (2007) estimates a rank dependent utility model that allows for a kink in the utility function at certainty. This may explain why their estimate of the curvature is lower than ours. Our estimates for the upper and lower quartiles for the η parameter were 1 . 0 and 0 . 35 respectively. 78 out of 103 (or 75 . 7 percent of subjects) have estimated η no greater than 1 and would thus satisfy stochastic impatience.

A striking feature in our estimates for the parametric model is that there is almost no correlation between estimated γ and estimated η . In the DEU model these coefficients are identical, which would imply a very high positive correlation coefficient; instead we find that it is -0 . 043.

## Appendix

## A.1 Well-behaved preorders and Proposition 1

In the paper we consider preorders defined on the consumption space R 4 + . Formally, a binary relation /trianglerightequal on R 4 + is a subset of R 4 + × R 4 + . We write a /trianglerightequal b if ( a, b ) ∈ /trianglerightequal . A preorder refers to a binary relation that is reflexive ( a /trianglerightequal a for all a ) and transitive ( a /trianglerightequal b and b /trianglerightequal c implies a /trianglerightequal c ). A preorder is said to be closed if /trianglerightequal , considered as a subset of R 4 + × R 4 + , is a closed set. The product order ≥ (where c ′ ≥ c if c ′ ij ≥ c ij for i = 1 , 2, j = 1 , 2) is a closed preorder.

Given a binary relation /trianglerightequal , we can define its transitive closure ; this is another binary relation on R 4 + , which we shall denote by /trianglerightequal + . By definition, a /trianglerightequal + b if there is a finite set of elements c j (for j = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) such that a /trianglerightequal c 1 , c 1 /trianglerightequal c 2 , and so forth until c m /trianglerightequal b . If /trianglerightequal is a reflexive binary relation, then /trianglerightequal + is a preorder.

Given two binary relations /trianglerightequal 1 and /trianglerightequal 2 , it its union is also a binary relation; we denote the transitive closure of this union by /trianglerightequal 1 · /trianglerightequal 2 ; we simply refer /trianglerightequal 1 · /trianglerightequal 2 as the transitive

closure of /trianglerightequal 1 and /trianglerightequal 2 . Note that if /trianglerightequal 1 and /trianglerightequal 2 are preorders, its union need not be a preorder, but its transitive closure will be.

Let ◦ denote the composition between two preorders /trianglerightequal 1 and /trianglerightequal 2 , so c /trianglerightequal 1 ◦ /trianglerightequal 2 c ′ if there exists ˜ c such that c /trianglerightequal 1 ˜ c /trianglerightequal 2 c ′ . Obviously, /trianglerightequal 1 ◦ /trianglerightequal 2 is a binary relation, but it need not be a preorder. A sufficient condition to guarantee that /trianglerightequal 1 ◦ /trianglerightequal 2 is a preorder is that the two preorders /trianglerightequal 1 and /trianglerightequal 2 commute , i.e., /trianglerightequal 1 ◦ /trianglerightequal 2 = /trianglerightequal 2 ◦ /trianglerightequal 1 . Note that a preorder /trianglerightequal is composable in the sense of Definition 4 if and only if it commutes with the ≥ preorder.

The following lemma on composable preorders (see Definition 4) has a completely straightforward proof which we shall omit.

Lemma 1. If /trianglerightequal is a closed and composable preorder then ≥ ◦ /trianglerightequal is a closed preorder.

Definition 13. A preorder /trianglerightequal on R 4 + is well-behaved if it is closed, composable, and c /trianglerightequal c ′ implies not c ′ &gt; c .

A utility function U : R 4 + → R is said to strictly agree (or extend ) the preorder /trianglerightequal if U ( c ′′ ) ≥ U ( c ′ ) whenever c ′′ /trianglerightequal c ′ and U ( c ′′ ) &gt; U ( c ′ ) whenever c ′′ /triangleright c ′ . Note that this notion is stronger than simply saying that U agrees with /trianglerightequal (see Definition 3). Our proof of Proposition 1 uses the following lemma.

Lemma 2. Let O = { ( c 1 , p 1 ) , . . . , ( c N , p N ) } be a dataset, let /trianglerightequal be a well-behaved preorder and let e &lt; e ∗ for e, e ∗ ∈ [0 , 1] . If O satisfies e ∗ -GARP/trianglerightequal then there is a utility function that strictly agrees with /trianglerightequal and e -rationalizes O .

Proof. Let e &lt; e ∗ and B n ( e ) = { c ∈ R 4 + : p n · c ≤ ep n · c n } . By Definition 2, the utility function U that we seek must satisfy U ( c n ) ≥ U ( c ) for all c ∈ B n ( e ). Since U is strictly increasing by definition, this is equivalent to U ( c n ) ≥ U ( c ) for all c ∈ ¯ B n ( e ), where

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

By Lemma 1, /trianglerightequal + = ≥ ◦ /trianglerightequal is a closed preorder. By Theorem 2 in NOQ, there is a function U that strictly agrees with /trianglerightequal + and satisfies U ( c n ) ≥ U ( c ) for all c ∈ ¯ B n ( e ) if the choices obey a sort of no-cycling condition. This is precisely what we need provided the following is true: (a) that if U strictly agrees with /trianglerightequal + then it is strictly increasing (and thus a true utility function in the sense defined in this paper) and strictly agrees with /trianglerightequal ; (b) the no-cycling condition in NOQ, when specialized to our context, is satisfied.

We first establish (a). Since U strictly agrees with /trianglerightequal + , U ( c ′′ ) ≥ U ( c ′ ) if c ′′ &gt; c ′ or c ′′ /triangleright c ′ , and U ( c ′′ ) = U ( c ′ ) only if c ′ /trianglerightequal + c ′′ (here c ′′ /triangleright c ′ means c ′′ /trianglerightequal c ′ and not c ′ /trianglerightequal c ′′ ). The latter condition means there is a ¯ c so that c ′ ≥ ¯ c /trianglerightequal c ′′ . Note that c ′ ≥ ¯ c /trianglerightequal c ′′

means not c ′′ &gt; c ′ (by /trianglerightequal being well-behaved). Now, suppose c ′′ /triangleright c ′ . Then we have c ′ ≥ ¯ c /trianglerightequal c ′′ /triangleright c ′ where the ≥ cannot be replaced by &gt; without contradicting the fact that /trianglerightequal is well-behaved and so it must be that c ′ = ¯ c /trianglerightequal c ′′ /triangleright c ′ which is a contradiction. We conclude that U ( c ′′ ) &gt; U ( c ′ ) if c ′′ &gt; c ′ or c ′′ /triangleright c ′ .

We now turn to claim (b). NOQ's condition is that e -GARP/trianglerightequal holds except that the revealed preference relations in NOQ are defined differently. We need to show that e ∗ -GARP/trianglerightequal guarantees that NOQ's e -GARP/trianglerightequal holds. In NOQ, c n is said to be e -revealed preferred to c m if there is ˜ c such that ˜ c ∈ ¯ B n ( e ) and ˜ c /trianglerightequal + c m , and c n is strictly e -revealed preferred to c m if ˜ c can be chosen such that ˜ c /triangleright + c m . Let us denote NOQ's revealed preference relations by /trianglerightequal /star e and /triangleright /star e .

It is easy to see that c n /trianglerightequal ∗ e ∗ c m if and only if c n /trianglerightequal /star e ∗ c m and so c n /trianglerightequal /star e c m implies c n /trianglerightequal ∗ e ∗ c m . It can also be shown easily that c n /triangleright ∗ e ∗ c m implies c n /triangleright /star e ∗ c m . However, c n /triangleright /star e ∗ c m does not imply c n /triangleright ∗ e ∗ c m . Yet, it is the case that c n /triangleright /star e c m implies c n /triangleright ∗ e ∗ c m which will be used to establish (b). Let us prove this claim. Suppose there exists ˜ c ∈ ¯ B n ( e ) so that ˜ c /triangleright + c m . By the composable property of /trianglerightequal this means there exists ¯ c /trianglerightequal c m so that ¯ c ∈ ¯ B n ( e ). This is equivalent to ¯ c /trianglerightequal c m and

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Since e ∗ &gt; e , we obtain c n /triangleright ∗ e ∗ c m . Now, suppose the data violate e ∗ -GARP/trianglerightequal with the /trianglerightequal ∗ e ∗ revealed preference relation. From what we have established about /trianglerightequal /star e , it follows that e -GARP/trianglerightequal is violated for the /trianglerightequal /star e revealed preference relation. Thus, e ∗ -GARP/trianglerightequal in the sense of Definition 8 implies e -GARP/trianglerightequal in NOQ's sense.

Proof of Proposition 1. We first show that if the dataset O can be rationalized by a utility function U that agrees with /trianglerightequal then the data will satisfy e -GARP/trianglerightequal . So, let U be a utility function that agrees with /trianglerightequal and rationalizes O at efficiency level e . Define ¯ B n ( e ) by (24). If c n /trianglerightequal ∗ e c m , then by definition there is ¯ c ∈ ¯ B n ( e ) such that ¯ c /trianglerightequal c m . This implies that U ( c n ) ≥ U (¯ c ) ≥ U ( c m ). If c n /triangleright ∗ e c m then we have, in addition, that U ( c n ) &gt; U (¯ c ). It follows from this that e -GARP/trianglerightequal holds. Thus

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

We must now establish the ' ≤ ' inequality part of the proof. Suppose e ∗ is the supremum value of all e so that O satisfies e -GARP/trianglerightequal . Lemma 2 shows that for any e &lt; e ∗ we may find a utility function U that strictly agrees with /trianglerightequal and rationalizes O at efficiency level e . As any U that strictly agrees with /trianglerightequal also agrees with /trianglerightequal , the supremum over all e such that O is e -rationalizable/trianglerightequal must be weakly greater than e ∗ .

## A.2 Testing Properties Jointly

Let /trianglerightequal 1 · /trianglerightequal 2 be the transitive closure of the preorders /trianglerightequal 1 and /trianglerightequal 2 . Suppose we would like to know if there is a utility function U that agrees with both /trianglerightequal 1 and /trianglerightequal 2 and rationalizes the choices of a participant at efficiency level e . This is clearly equivalent to asking if the choices are e -rationalizable-( /trianglerightequal 1 · /trianglerightequal 2 ). The following property, possessed by all preorders considered in this paper, makes it possible to test this condition.

Definition 14. A preorder /trianglerightequal is an arranging preorder if (¯ c 1 , ¯ c 2 , ¯ c 3 , ¯ c 4 ) /trianglerightequal ( c 1 , c 2 , c 3 , c 4 ) implies ¯ c k ∈ { c 1 , c 2 , c 3 , c 4 } for each k .

Lemma 3. If /trianglerightequal 1 and /trianglerightequal 2 are composable and arranging preorders, then /trianglerightequal 1 · /trianglerightequal 2 is a composable and arranging preorder.

Proof. Clearly /trianglerightequal 1 · /trianglerightequal 2 is arranging. Suppose c ≥ ◦ ( /trianglerightequal 1 · /trianglerightequal 2 ) c ′ . Then it must be the case that c ≥ ◦ /trianglerightequal 1 ◦ /trianglerightequal 2 ◦ /trianglerightequal 1 ◦ /trianglerightequal 2 . . . /trianglerightequal 1 ◦ /trianglerightequal 2 c ′ . By applying the composable property repeatedly, we see c ( /trianglerightequal 1 · /trianglerightequal 2 ) ◦ ≥ c ′ .

Proposition 2. Suppose /trianglerightequal 1 and /trianglerightequal 2 are well-behaved and arranging preorders and suppose the following property holds: c ( /trianglerightequal 1 · /trianglerightequal 2 ) c ′ implies not c ′ &gt; c . Then /trianglerightequal 1 · /trianglerightequal 2 is a well-behaved and arranging preorder.

Proof. By Lemma 3 we see that /trianglerightequal 1 · /trianglerightequal 2 is composable and arranging. So, to show that /trianglerightequal 1 · /trianglerightequal 2 is well-behaved we must show that it is closed.

For a binary relation /trianglerightequal and integer K let /trianglerightequal K denote the binary relation formed by composing /trianglerightequal with itself K times. As /trianglerightequal 1 and /trianglerightequal 2 are arranging it must be the case that there is a K so that /trianglerightequal 1 · /trianglerightequal 2 = ( /trianglerightequal 2 ◦ /trianglerightequal 1 ) K . Thus, it suffices to show for two closed and arranging preorders /trianglerightequal 1 and /trianglerightequal 2 that /trianglerightequal 2 ◦ /trianglerightequal 1 is closed. Suppose we have a sequence c k → c and c ′ k → c ′ so that c k /trianglerightequal 2 ◦ /trianglerightequal 1 c ′ k for each k . So, there exists a sequence ¯ c k so that c k /trianglerightequal 2 ¯ c k /trianglerightequal 1 c ′ k . We may assume that ¯ c k → ¯ c (or at least we could create such a subsequence). As /trianglerightequal 1 and /trianglerightequal 2 are closed we see c /trianglerightequal 2 ¯ c /trianglerightequal 1 c ′ .

In order to check if a dataset is consistent with two properties (such as lottery equivalence and impatience) jointly, we would need to apply Proposition 1 to the preorder that is the transitive closure of two preorders. In this situation, Proposition 2 is useful because it spells out the condition under which the transitive closure of well-behaved and arranging preorders is also well-behaved and arranging.

## A.3 Our menagerie of preorders

Except for the elementary results relying on Afriat's Theorem in Section 5.1, all the results in Section 5 rely on applications of Proposition 1, with different preorders chosen for different tests. We know that a utility function satisfies lottery equivalence if and only if it agrees with /trianglerightequal LE and impatience if and only if it agrees with /trianglerightequal I . Given these, there are obvious analogous preorders /trianglerightequal TN and /trianglerightequal P that characterize time neutrality and patience. Other properties studied in Section 5 can be characterized in a similar way. The following claims are easy to check:

- )).

(1) A utility function satisfies correlation neutrality if and only if it agrees with /trianglerightequal CN , where /trianglerightequal CN is defined as follows: for all a , b , a ′ , and b ′ ≥ 0, we have (( a, b ) , ( a ′ , b ′ )) /trianglerightequal CN (( a ′ , b ) , ( a, b ′ )), (( a, b ) , ( a ′ , b ′ )) /trianglerightequal CN ( a, b ′ ) , ( a ′ , b )), and (( a, b ) , ( a ′ , b ′ )) /trianglerightequal CN (( a ′ , b ′ ) , ( a, b (2) A utility function satisfies correlation aversion if and only if it agrees with /trianglerightequal LE and with /trianglerightequal CA , where /trianglerightequal CA is defined as follows: if a ≥ a ′ and b ≥ b ′ , then (( a ′ , b ) , ( a, b ′ )) /trianglerightequal CA (( a, b ) , ( a ′ , b ′ )). The correlation loving property can be characterized by requiring the utility function to agree with /trianglerightequal LE and with /trianglerightequal CL , where the latter is defined as follows: c ′ /trianglerightequal CL c ′′ if c ′′ /trianglerightequal CA c ′ .

(3) A utility function satisfies stochastic impatience if and only if it agrees with /trianglerightequal LE and with /trianglerightequal SI , where /trianglerightequal SI is defined as follows: for all d ′ , d ′′ , d ≤ b ≤ a where d = max( d ′ , d ′′ ), we have (( a, d ) , ( d, b )) /trianglerightequal SI (( b, d ′ ) , ( d ′′ , a )). Autility function satisfies stochastic patience if it agrees with /trianglerightequal LE and with /trianglerightequal SP , with the latter defined as follows: for all d ′ , d ′′ , d ≤ b ≤ a where d = max( d ′ , d ′′ ), we have (( b, d ) , ( d, a )) /trianglerightequal SP (( a, d ′ ) , ( d ′′ , b )).

It is straightforward to check that every preorder we consider, i.e., every preorder in the set

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

is well-behaved and arranging. Thus by Proposition 2, the transitive closure of the union of any subset of these preorders is also well-behaved and arranging. For example, in Section 5.3, when we tested correlation aversion, we maintained lottery equivalence and impatience as background assumptions. A utility function will have these properties if and only if it agrees with the preorder /trianglerightequal = /trianglerightequal LE · /trianglerightequal I · /trianglerightequal CA . This preorder is again wellbehaved. Thus, according to Proposition 1, we can calculate the efficiency index for the joint { lottery equivalence + impatience + correlation aversion } property by checking e -GARP according to /trianglerightequal .

## A.4 Implementing the Tests

Here we discuss how to actually test if the behavior of a subject satisfies e -GARP/trianglerightequal . We shall focus on the case where we seek to test e -GARP/trianglerightequal for some /trianglerightequal = /trianglerightequal 1 · /trianglerightequal 2 however our method shall easily generalize to the case of combining K preorders, /trianglerightequal = /trianglerightequal 1 · /trianglerightequal 2 · . . . · /trianglerightequal K .

We shall suppose that the preorders /trianglerightequal 1 and /trianglerightequal 2 are well-behaved and arranging, so that for any c the sets I /trianglerightequal 1 ( c ) and I /trianglerightequal 2 ( c ) are finite and easy to compute.

Here is a procedure which will check for e -GARP/trianglerightequal .

## Procedure.

Step 1. For each m calculate the set { c ∈ R 4 + : c /trianglerightequal c m } .

Step 2. For each n and m determine if c n /trianglerightequal ∗ e c m .

Step 3. Determine if there is a sequence n 1 , . . . , n K so that c n 1 /trianglerightequal ∗ e

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Step 3 in the Procedure can be accomplished using Warshall's algorithm (an algorithm for computing the transitive closure of a graph.) So, the challenge lies in Steps 1 and 2.

To accomplish Step 1 we shall construct a sequence of sets

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

whose elements are in R 4 + . 38 Let V 0 = { c m } and let V 1 , 1 = { c ∈ R 4 + : c /trianglerightequal 1 ¯ c, where ¯ c ∈ V 0 } . Let V 1 , 2 = { c ∈ R 4 + : c /trianglerightequal 2 ¯ c, where ¯ c ∈ V 1 , 1 } . And, for k &gt; 1 let

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Each of these sets is computable since I /trianglerightequal 1 ( c m ) and I /trianglerightequal 2 ( c m ) can be computed. Because /trianglerightequal is arranging there will be a k &gt; 1 so that V k, 2 = V k -1 , 2 . It is easy to show that V k, 2 = { c ∈ R 4 + : c /trianglerightequal c m } and so we have constructed the desired set.

Once Step 1 is completed Step 2 is easy. Merely, consider each bundle ¯ c ∈ { c ∈ R 4 + : c /trianglerightequal c m } and consider if ep n · c n ≥ p n · ¯ c . If the answer is 'yes' for any ¯ c then c n /trianglerightequal ∗ e c m and otherwise it is not the case that c n /trianglerightequal ∗ e c m .

38 The V stands for 'visited.'

## A.5 Separability and Correlation Aversion

Here we discuss the connection between correlation aversion and the separable structures on preferences. Let X = ( X 0 , X 1 , . . . , X T ) denote a T +1 dimensional random vector over some finite sample space with S elements. Let us write X 1 , . . . , X S for the S non-risky temporal streams of consumption. X s is the consumption stream realized, over T + 1 periods, should state s occur (so, X s ∈ R T +1 + ). A utility function U is state-separable if X is awarded utility

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

for some increasing aggregator function F S and increasing sub-utility function G S . We say that U is time-separable if X is awarded utility

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

for some increasing aggregator function F T and increasing sub-utility function G T (whose domain consists of the random variables X t ). Note that the DEU model is both stateseparable and time-separable while a utility function that allows for correlation aversion cannot be time-separable.

A discussion found in Andreoni and Sprenger (2012a), Epper and Fehr-Duda (2015), and Andreoni et al. (2017) concerns whether utility should be time-separable or stateseparable. Andreoni et al. (2017) observe that with the time-separable formulation, an agent's preference over lotteries that pay out in period t are unaffected by the presence of lotteries that pay out independently in some other time period t ′ &gt; t ; they refer to this property as common future risk invariance (CFRI). Andreoni et al. (2017) find some support for this behavior in their experiment.

It is in fact possible to reconcile correlation aversion with a restricted version of time-separability. For example, suppose that an agent has the model

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

This function 39 is obviously state-separable and it is not difficult to check that it obeys correlation aversion (when states are equiprobable), so it could potentially explain the behavior typically displayed by subjects in our experiment. On the other hand, the model is also time-separable when X 0 , . . . , X T are independent (which follows from the

39 A similar functional form can be found in Richard (1975).

fact that E [ Z 1 Z 2 ] = E [ Z 1 ] E [ Z 2 ] when Z 1 and Z 2 are independent random variables), and thus the agent could also display CFRI.

## A.6 Stochastic Impatience

Proposition 3. Suppose that U has the form of (13) , where φ and u are both strictly increasing and u (0) = 0 (so u ( r ) &gt; 0 for all r &gt; 0 ). Then U displays stochastic impatience if Φ as defined by (18) is a convex function. Conversely, if Φ is not a convex function, then there is a strictly increasing and concave function u , with u (0) = 0 , such that U with the form (13) violates stochastic dominance.

The proof of this result requires the following lemma.

which is the inequality we want.

## A.7 Ordinal Dominance

Proposition 4. If the utility function U : R 4 + → R satisfies ordinal dominance, then it can be expressed in the weakly separable form (10) , where f is given by (19) and G is increasing, continuous, and symmetric. Conversely, if U has the form (10), where f is increasing and continuous and G is increasing, continuous, and symmetric, then U satisfies ordinal dominance.

Proof. Suppose U satisfies ordinal dominance. Let f : R 2 + → R be defined by (19). Since f is continuous and strictly increasing, its range R ( f ) = { f ( a, b ) : ( a, b ) ∈ R 2 + }

coincides with { f ( z, z ) : z ∈ R + } . The function G : R ( r ) → R is defined by

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

for all a , b ∈ R + . To verify that this definition is consistent note that f is one-to-one on the restricted domain of points of the form ( z, z ) ∈ R 2 + . Let us verify that (10) holds. Let (( a, b ) , ( a ′ , b ′ )) ∈ R 4 + be an arbitrary bundle. By the fact that f is continuous and increasing there exists d and d ′ so that f ( a, b ) = f ( d, d ) and f ( a ′ , b ′ ) = f ( d ′ , d ′ ). As U satisfies ordinal dominance we have U (( a, b ) , ( a ′ , b ′ )) = U (( d, d ) , ( a ′ , b ′ )) = U (( d, d ) , ( d ′ , d ′ )). Finally, we see from our definition of G that U (( a, b ) , ( a ′ , b ′ )) = G ( f ( a, b ) , ( a ′ , b ′ )) which confirms that U has a weakly separable representation.

Suppose U has a representation of the form in (10) where f is increasing and continuous and G is increasing, continuous, and symmetric. We prove that U satisfies ordinal dominance. The fact that G is symmetric ensures that U satisfies lottery equivalence. Next, suppose we have U (( a, b ) , ( a, b )) ≥ U (( a ′ , b ′ ) , ( a ′ , b ′ )) for some a, a ′ , b, b ′ . As f and g are increasing we obtain f ( a, b ) ≥ f ( a ′ , b ′ ). Let a ′′ and b ′′ be non-negative numbers; the fact that g and f are increasing guarantees that U (( a, b ) , ( a ′′ , b ′′ )) = G ( f ( a, b ) , f ( a ′′ , b ′′ )) ≥ G ( f ( a ′ , b ′ ) , f ( a ′′ , b ′′ )) = U (( a ′ , b ′ ) , ( a ′′ , b ′′ )) as required.
